text: Welcome to this video which documents a robotic left-sided excision of a bronchogenic cyst with sutured repair. This was done at James Cook University Hospital by Mr. Joel Dunning.

We have no conflicts of interest to declare. This patient is a thirty-five-year-old lady that had reported experiencing an intermittent and reedy productive cough for several years. It was very offensive and troublesome in nature.

She also reported intermittent chest infections over the years. This is her CT scan and we've stopped at her bronchogenic cyst, which is just where the trachea bifurcates into the left and right main bronchi.
The preoperative bronchoscopy reveals a small hole located a couple of centimeters distal to the carina on the left side, positioned at approximately the two o'clock orientation.

If we gave a bit of positive pressure ventilation, that opened up, and this was the real trouble, because there is a big cyst behind this, and this cyst closes up and opens again, and fills full of offensive productive sputum, that she then coughs up intermittently.
The video displays the standardized Cifolia V port technique for robotic surgery, showing the specific arrangement and placement of surgical ports on the patient's left side.
The surgeon uses a five millimeter thoracic grasper positioned anteriorly on the left side, while the camera is placed more posteriorly to provide a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon holds a Maryland dissector with bipolar energy in the right hand and a soft-tipped Catiae forceps in the left hand, preparing to manipulate tissue during the thymectomy procedure.
A swab is used in the chest cavity for safety and cleaning small amounts of blood. The surgeon dissects the inferior pulmonary ligament, progressing past the pulmonary vein.

The left pulmonary artery is visible, with the surgeon gently grasping the vagus nerve to preserve it. The pulmonary branches of the vagus nerve are seen below the cariae forceps, and these branches are divided as the vagus nerve is retracted posteriorly.
The surgeon dissects down the left main bronchus, revealing the cyst sac adjacent to the bronchial structure.
The collapsed cyst is visible, likely due to its connection to the airways, which are under negative pressure during one-lung ventilation with suction applied.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to expose the right main bronchus, visible in the surgical field. The instrument is then switched to scissors to achieve clean, non-diathermed edges on the bronchus, minimizing potential damage.
The surgeon opens the cyst, revealing its depth of spread, and carefully washes out the interior to minimize contamination of the operative field, which appears clean.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to perform the final excision of the cyst, revealing a small hole in the membranous portion of the left main bronchus. The interior of the cystic cavity appears relatively clean, resembling the preoperative bronchoscopic view.
This was then sent for histology, which I imagine will show a bronchogenic cyst.
The surgeon mobilizes a small amount of mediastinal tissue to cover the hole in the bronchus, considering the placement of a pericardial patch for reinforcement.
The surgeon uses a piece of mediastinal tissue to cover the small hole in the bronchus, securing it with a single 4-0 Vicryl suture to minimize the use of prosthetic material on the chest.
The surgeon places two stitches into the membranous part of the bronchus, securing them over the defect. The sutures are expected to be absorbed within two months.
The surgeon applies a small amount of Evisil glue over the wound site for additional security, followed by the placement of a surgical drain to manage fluid accumulation.
The surgeon performs a series of intercostal blocks and a phrenic nerve block at the start of the procedure, without placing an extra pleural catheter.
The surgeon gently inflates the lungs using a pressure of twenty centimeters of water, resulting in full lung inflation, indicating the completion of the cyst excision.
The patient should recover from this procedure very rapidly with minimal post-operative pain. Thank you very much for watching and please feel free to reach out to us if you have any questions.
